metadata.title: Few-Shot: Examples Encode What Words Can't
sources: Anthropic Prompt Engineering Interactive Tutorial — Chapter 7: Using Examples (Few-Shot Prompting)

beats.narration_text: The claim that examples encode what instructions can't finally made few-shot click for me, but I want to feel the boundary. Take a formatting task where the rule is genuinely hard to write in words, like matching an idiosyncratic date or citation style: (1) show me the same task attempted with only a written instruction and where it drifts, (2) then supply three examples and point out exactly which pattern the examples pinned down that words missed, (3) tell me how many examples is too few versus wasteful.

props.command: The claim that examples encode what instructions can't finally made few-shot click for me, but I want to feel the boundary. Take a formatting task where the rule is genuinely hard to write in words, like matching an idiosyncratic date or citation style: (1) show me the same task attempted with only a written instruction and where it drifts, (2) then supply three examples and point out exactly which pattern the examples pinned down that words missed, (3) tell me how many examples is too few versus wasteful.
output: Examples encode implicit style rules that are nearly impossible to specify
Tone, register, and domain vocabulary transfer directly from examples
Zero-shot vs few-shot: quality difference is measurable on the same task
Format templates are extracted from examples — no schema required

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The parent bot example from the tutorial: ask Claude 'Will Santa bring me presents on Christmas?' with no examples. You get a formal, measured response — reasonable, accurate, and absolutely devastating to a six-year-old. Now feed it one example: a question about the tooth fairy answered in a warm, playful parent voice. Same Santa question. Now Claude responds like a parent. One example moved the entire register.

props.artifactHeading: One example rewrites the entire register.
artifactLines: Zero-shot: 'Santa Claus is a cultural figure associated with...' — technically correct
One-shot example: tooth fairy → warm, playful, parent voice
Same Santa question: Claude now matches the example tone exactly
No instruction could achieve this in one sentence
The example encodes implicit rules: vocabulary, warmth level, sentence length

beats.narration_text: Format replication: the name extraction example. Feed Claude a paragraph, then a formatted list — name and role in brackets. Do it twice. Then give Claude a third paragraph and ask for the list. It extracts and formats correctly without a format specification. Claude reverse-engineered the template from two examples and applied it. This is the real power: you don't describe the schema, you show it.

props.artifactHeading: Show the structure twice — Claude extracts the template.
artifactLines: Input: two (paragraph → formatted list) pairs as examples
Third paragraph: no instructions, just the input text
Output: Claude produces the exact same list format
Template was reverse-engineered from examples — no schema needed
Works for any repeating structured output: reports, cards, records

beats.narration_text: Zero-shot versus one-shot versus three-shot on the same task. Zero-shot: Claude applies its own defaults. One-shot: noticeable shift toward the example's style. Three-shot: the pattern locks in — variability drops, output consistency rises. More shots generally help, but diminishing returns set in. Three to five examples cover most cases. The first example does the heaviest lifting.

props.artifactTitle: Zero vs One vs Three Shots
props.artifactHeading: Each example reduces variability and locks the pattern.
artifactLines: 0-shot: Claude defaults — correct but generic, your style absent
1-shot: noticeable register shift toward example's style
3-shot: pattern locks, output consistency rises measurably
Diminishing returns after 5 examples for most tasks
First example does the heaviest lifting — choose it carefully

beats.narration_text: When NOT to use few-shot. If the task is fully specifiable in clear instructions — 'return only the city name, nothing else' — then examples add noise, not signal. Also skip it when examples are hard to source or don't exist yet. Few-shot works best when the requirement has a subjective feel that instructions can't capture: voice, brand tone, editorial judgment. If you can write the rule down precisely, write it down.

props.artifactHeading: Explicit instructions beat examples when the rule is precise.
artifactLines: Skip few-shot if the output rule is fully specifiable in words
Example: 'return only the city name' — instruction is cleaner
Skip if examples are hard to source or don't exist yet
Best fit: subjective feel — voice, brand tone, editorial judgment
Rule of thumb: if you can write it precisely, write it

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Ask Claude: 'Here are three examples of how our team writes status updates: [paste three real examples]. Now write a status update for this: [describe a task].' The examples do the work. Claude reads the three examples and extracts your team's implicit rules — sentence length, level of detail, whether you use bullet points, how formal the language is. None of that had to be explained. That's why few-shot prompting is the right tool when your output has a feel that can't be fully written down. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Here are three examples of how our team writes status updates: [examples]. Now write one for: [task].
props.segment: Few-Shot: Examples Encode What Words Can't
output: Claude extracts sentence length, detail level, and formality from examples
Your team's implicit style rules transfer without being written down
Output matches feel — not just format — of the example set

beats.narration_text: Few-Shot: Examples Encode What Words Can't
